title: Nonlinear phonon dispersion in disordered solids and non-Debye vibrational spectra
abstract: All solids, whether crystalline or disordered, support elastic wave propagation with a linear dispersion relation in the long-wavelength limit. These waves, corresponding to low-frequency phonons, feature a vibrational density of states that follows Debye's classical model. Deviations from Debye's predictions with increasing frequency can emerge from phonon dispersion nonlinearity and from non-phononic vibrational modes, which exist in non-crystalline solids due to structural disorder. Both nonlinear phonon dispersion in disordered solids and its relative contribution to non-Debye anomalies, most notably manifested by the controversial boson peak, remain poorly understood. Here we show that nonlinear phonon dispersion in a broad range of disordered solids, including elastic networks and various glasses, emerge from a mesoscopic, disorder-induced lengthscale, which also controls wave attenuation. We subsequently use analysis and large-scale computer simulations to quantitatively determine the relative contributions of nonlinear phonon softening and non-phononic vibrations to the onset of non-Debye anomalies and to the boson peak. We show that the relative magnitude of the two contributions strongly depends on the strength of disorder of the solid, e.g., controlled by the thermal history upon glass formation, and that for realistic laboratory glasses both pieces of physics significantly contribute to the boson peak. These findings constitute basic progress in understanding disordered solids.
summary: This paper provides a unified framework to understand how disorder-induced mesoscopic lengthscales drive both nonlinear phonon dispersion and wave attenuation in glasses and networks. It quantitatively demonstrates that the Boson peak is a hybrid phenomenon resulting from both softened phonons and non-phononic vibrations. The findings offer a way to interpret experimental vibrational spectra based on the level of structural disorder.
main_problem: The paper aims to determine the relative contributions of nonlinear phonon dispersion (phonon softening) and non-phononic vibrational modes to the non-Debye vibrational spectra, specifically the Boson peak, in disordered solids.
main_result: The authors show that the Boson peak arises from both mechanisms, with the relative importance depending on the degree of disorder; they also identify a mesoscopic, disorder-induced lengthscale that governs both nonlinear dispersion and wave attenuation.
method: The authors use a new 'imposed-wave method' to calculate phonon dispersion in disordered systems and perform large-scale computer simulations using the Kernel Polynomial Method and direct diagonalization.
model_or_system: The study covers a broad range of disordered solids, including disordered elastic networks, repulsive polydisperse glasses, repulsive binary glasses, and sticky-sphere binary glasses.
key_observables: Phonon dispersion relation omega(k), vibrational density of states (VDoS) D(omega), reduced VDoS, Boson peak frequency, wave attenuation rate, and the disorder-induced lengthscale xi.
important_parameters: Coordination number (z), thermal history (Tp), interaction range (rc), disorder quantifier (chi), and the interatomic distance (a0).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes harmonic vibrations described by the eigenvalues of the Hessian matrix.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows phonon dispersion softening and scaling with the lengthscale xi; Figure 2 shows the scaling of xi with various disorder parameters; Figure 3 compares VDoS in crystals vs disordered solids; Figure 5 validates wave-speed fits; Figure 6 shows extraction of the non-phononic VDoS prefactor.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-Debye spectra, presents the imposed-wave method, demonstrates the emergence of a mesoscopic lengthscale xi, quantifies the relative contributions of phonon softening and non-phononic modes to the Boson peak, and concludes by addressing contradictions in recent literature regarding dispersion relations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phonon dispersion relations of disordered solids. (a) The shear (transverse) phonon dispersion relation ωs(k) obtained using the ‘imposed-wave method’ (see Appendix B 1 for details) for 21 computer disordered solids of three different classes (9 disordered elastic networks of different topologies quantified by an average degree of connectivity z, 8 polydisperse glasses of different thermal histories upon glass formation quantified by Tp and 4 binary glasses of different strengths of interatomic attractive forces quantified by rc, see text and Appendix A). ωs is normalized by cs/a0, where cs is the shear wave-speed, and the wavenumber k is normalized by 1/a0. The linear approximation...

FIG. 2. A disorder-induced dispersion lengthscale. (a) ξ/a0 (as obtained in Fig. 1) vs. 1/(z−zc) for the disordered elastic networks considered therein. The superposed line and the power-law triangle highlight the scaling relation ξ(z)∼1/√z−zc, over the entire range of z values used, covering more than two orders of magnitude in δz =z−zc. (b) ξ/a0 (as obtained in Fig. 1) vs. Tp for the polydisperse glasses considered therein, formed under a wide range of thermal histories. (c) ξ/a0 (as obtained in Fig. 1) vs. rc for the binary glasses considered therein, featuring different strengths of interatomic attractive forces. (d) ξ/a0 vs. χ for all disordered solids considered in panels (a)-(c),...

FIG. 3. The onset of non-Debye vibrational spectra. (a) The reduced VDoS D(ω)/ADω2 vs. ω/ωD for a monoatomic face-centered cubic (FCC) crystal. Debye’s plateau level, corresponding to unity in this presentation, is marked by the horizontal dashed line. The reduced VDoS features a non-Debye behavior with increasing frequency ω and a peak corresponding to a Van Hove singularity/cusp, see text for discussion. (inset) The VDoS D(ω) used in the main panel. (b) The same as panel (a), but for a disordered elastic network. The frequency ω⋆above which non-phononic vibrations emerge (see text and Appendix D) is marked by the vertical dashed-dotted line. The reduced VDoS features a non-Debye behavior...

FIG. 4. The different contributions to the boson peak. (a) The cumulative VDoS C(ω)= R ω 0 D(ω′)dω′ (solid line) for the Tp =0.32 glass analyzed in Fig. 2b (the most deeply supercooled one). The vertical dashed line marks the BP frequency ωBP, which is determined using the reduced VDoS shown in the inset. Superposed are Debye’s cumulative VDoS CD(ω) (dashed curve) and the cumulative phononic VDoS Cph(ω) (dashed-dotted curve). The shaded area provides an estimate of the number of non-phononic vibrations, see text. FIG. 5. Validation of wave-speed fits. The shear (cs, panel (a)) and longitudinal (cℓ, panel (b)) wave speeds as obtained from the cubic dispersion fits (see Tables I, II and IV), plotted against direct computations obtained via the elastic moduli of our model solids. An excellent agreement is observed.
